title: EXPAND Keep It Between The Lines
original_prose:
“It’s the hurt I hide that fuels the fire inside me”

— Sturgill Simpson, Keep It Between the Lines

####

[CLEARANCE GRANTED — SYNRGY STRATEGIC RESPONSE COMMAND | ORIGIN: CENTRAL STRATEGIC AUTHORITY]TO: 6TH BOMBARDMENT WING, SYNRGY ONE COMMAND — LAS VEGAS

Escalation protocol approved.

Full-spectrum CORE deployment authorized for gridpoint DEN-04.

Justification: sustained broadcast subversion, confirmed rebel sanctuary, and high-value dissident presence.

Civil unrest now exceeds acceptable noncompliant yield thresholds.

Proceed with reclamation.

— Gen. A. K. Sorensen, Strategic Response Command



####

The void’s quiet now.

Not dead. Just... resting.

He’s across from me. Legs folded. Watching.

“You had it all,” he says.

I nod. “Yeah.”

“You were happy.”

“For a night.”

“And then?”

I breathe in. Slow. “Then there was chaos.”

He doesn’t flinch. Just nods.

“But you had it.”

“I did,” I say. “I don't know if that means anything."

The void doesn’t argue.

It just waits.



####

I wake up warm.

Rock beneath me. Her scent still in the blanket. Smoke. Sweat. Apple skin. That soft noise she makes when she dreams and doesn’t know I’m listening.

I reach for her.

Nothing.

The space is empty. Cooling.

Then the sirens start.

Not the natural kind. Not wind or birds or even wolves. Metal. Digital. Sliced from the throat of some old surveillance array.

I sit up fast. Too fast. My head spins. The whole canyon hums.

Voices rise—confused. Panicked. Someone yells from the upper tiers.

"They’re bombing again?"

No.

They’re not bombing again.

I grab a scope from a young girl frozen near the camp's edge. Scan the sky.

Six dots. High. Sleek. Fast.

Not bombers.

CORE platforms.

Heavily rigged. Triple drop bays. Geodesic belly flares. Meant to flatten entire gridpoints without blinking.

Not over Denver this time.

They’re coming here.

I shout. Don't remember the words. Something about shelter. Underground. Now.

Most don’t listen. Some do. The smart ones. The scared ones. The ones with kids.

Then I see them.

Behind the COREs—two dozen more. Slow. Fat. Carrier class.

Troop drops.

WRAITH carriers.

I feel my stomach hollow. I’ve seen those in orbit. I’ve seen what they birth. I've led the WRAITHS out of the bays.

Where the fuck is she?

I turn. Scan the ridgelines. The gates. The main tunnel.

And then—

She’s there.

Walking toward me through the mess like she knew this was coming all along.

Barefoot. Calm. A rifle slung over one shoulder, a bag of mags in one hand. My sword in the other.

She stops in front of me. Tosses me the gear.

"Time to get back on the clock, babe."

I catch the rifle on instinct.

"There’s no defending this," I say. "Not from what’s coming."

She shrugs. That grin like thunder behind glass.

"We have to try."

She turns. Doesn’t wait.

And I follow.



####

The first blast hits before we reach the amphitheater.

It doesn’t sound like thunder. It sounds like the sky being peeled open.

The shockwave cracks the ridge. Sends a ripple through the stone. People scream. Someone vomits. Dust rains from the cliffs like ash.

Kat doesn’t flinch. Just tightens the sling on her rifle and pushes forward.

I stay with her. Half a step behind. Always behind.

We move fast. Through tunnels carved by hand and spite. Into the dark.

The rebels are scrambling—pulling children into hollows, tossing gear down shafts, slapping awake the ones too high to move. No drills. No protocol. Just panic and noise.

We hit the first junction and it’s already overrun.

Two WRAITHs. Black plates. No eyes.

Rebels are shooting. Their bullets are useless.

"Save your bullets for flesh! Steel only on these fuckers!"

Her rifle stays slung. She slides under one, then up in front of it like magic, her blade spinning through its neck.

"No cores! Heads only!"

I swing right. Blade up. Mine hums again, just a little. Like it remembers what it was built for.

I take one through the neck. Kat finishes the other.

No words. Just breath. Move.

We push deeper. Flickering lights. Half-collapsed walls. Smoke stings the eyes. Shouts from somewhere below—close, then gone.

We hit a bottleneck near the old staging pit. Four more WRAITHs. One has a flamethrower rig. The other three move like they’ve been here before.

Kat doesn’t hesitate.

She slides under the first blast, pops up, and jams a blade into the carrier’s gut. A blade I didn’t know she still had.

I take the high ground. Leap from a broken scaffold and bring my sword down on one mid-turn. Cleaves through armor like paper. Hot blood or coolant or something worse hits my face.

Another WRAITH grabs me from behind. Kat shoots it in the face.

We fall into each other.

“Nice timing,” I breathe.

She grins. “Don’t get used to it.”

We keep moving.

We lose two fighters near the cistern. Echo kids. Young. Brave. Stupid. One tries to block a door with her body. The other screams until the ceiling falls.

Kat keeps count. I don’t.

By the time we reach the heart of the caverns—the old auditorium—there’s barely anyone left.

They pour in behind us. WRAITHs. Dozens. Maybe hundreds. Silent. Efficient. A choir of steel and ruin.

We make our stand there.

One exit behind us. Rubble to the sides. We fight on stairs carved by rebels who thought this would be a chapel.

It’s not a chapel.

It’s a tomb.

Kat’s blade is slick. Her arms covered in soot and blood. Her braid’s half undone.

Mine hums again. Steady now.

We fight like we’ve done this before.

And we have.

She cuts wide. I close gaps. She drops low. I leap high. We’re rhythm. Pattern. Holy ruin.

For a minute—just one—we almost win.

Then they bring in the crush rigs.

Sonic pulses. Shock grenades. Something chemical.

My ears bleed. My knees give.

Kat stumbles. A WRAITH gets close—too close. She stabs it in the thigh, but another’s behind it. Two more flank.

I try to reach her.

Can’t.

She goes down hard. Doesn’t scream.

She turns her head toward me. Eyes lock.

“Don’t let them bury what we built,” she says.

Then she’s gone.

I rage. Swing wild. Blade connects with something. Someone.

Then pain.

Fire in my back. Knees again. Concrete. Blood.

I fall.

And the dark takes me.



Booke Three - SIgnalfall
